answers.field_label: Times Definitely Not Available For Duty
answers.value: I work a regular daytime schedule at my primary job on Tuesdays and Thursdays from 07:30 to 17:00, so during those hours I am generally not available for duty except in the case of a major emergency and with advance coordination with my supervisor. In addition, on alternating Wednesday evenings from 18:00 to 20:30 I attend a continuing education course at Lackawanna College in Scranton, which I am committed to through the end of the current semester; during that specific window it is difficult for me to respond unless I receive at least several hours notice to adjust. I also reserve Sunday mornings between 08:30 and 11:30 for church and family commitments; while I could occasionally flex this if absolutely necessary, I would prefer not to be scheduled as primary duty crew during that exact time block. Outside of those recurring obligations, I am generally flexible and willing to pick up both day and night shifts, including holidays, and I am accustomed to being on call overnight with my phone nearby and my vehicle fueled and packed with my work bag so I can leave quickly when toned out.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: Completed a two-year Emergency Medical Services certification program (EMT-Basic to Advanced EMT bridge) at Lackawanna College in Scranton, PA, which is structured similarly to a vocational/technical program with a strong emphasis on clinical rotations, field internships, and hands-on skills labs, including extensive time spent riding third with local ambulance services and in the emergency department at Wayne Memorial Hospital.

answers.value: Karen O’Donnell, RN, BSN – ED Nurse Manager
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: Currently employed. I started as a full-time Emergency Department Technician in March 2020 and remain in this position. I am listing this here because it is my primary current job. Over time my responsibilities have expanded from basic patient transport and vital signs to include performing 12-lead EKGs, assisting with splinting and wound care, setting up for intubations, and supporting RNs and physicians during trauma activations. I have not left this job; in fact, my interest in joining the Hawley EMS membership/employment program is directly related to the clinical exposure and experience I have gained here, and my supervisor is supportive of me taking on EMS shifts as long as they do not interfere with my scheduled ED hours.

answers.value: Michael Russo, LPN – Resident Care Coordinator
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: I worked as a Resident Care Aide (PCA) while I was in my EMS training program. My primary duties included assisting residents with activities of daily living (bathing, dressing, toileting, and mobility), monitoring and documenting vital signs for residents with higher acuity needs, reporting changes in condition to the nursing staff, and providing emotional support to residents and their families. I left this position in February 2020 when I was offered a full-time role at Wayne County Regional Medical Center in the Emergency Department. The move allowed me to align my work more closely with my long-term goal of working in emergency services and provided more opportunities for hands-on patient care in an acute setting, as well as better pay and benefits. I left Pine Grove on very good terms, provided a full month’s notice, and I am still in occasional contact with some of my former coworkers and residents’ families.

answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: This was my first long-term job after high school. I started as a hostess and eventually became a server and shift lead. My responsibilities included greeting and seating guests, handling cash and credit card transactions, training new front-of-house staff, and coordinating with the kitchen to ensure timely service. I learned a lot about customer service, multitasking under pressure, and de-escalating tense situations with dissatisfied customers—skills that have translated surprisingly well to dealing with anxious patients and family members in healthcare settings. I left in August 2016 when I began my patient care aide training at Pine Grove Assisted Living and needed a more predictable schedule and a job more directly connected to healthcare. I provided notice and worked through the busy summer season before transitioning out.

answers.value: Part-time cashier and deli clerk while I was in my last two years of high school. I handled the register, stocked shelves, prepared simple deli orders, and helped with closing duties such as reconciling the till and cleaning the prep area. I left in August 2014 to focus on working more hours at Riverbend Family Restaurant, which offered more consistent evening and weekend shifts and slightly higher pay, as well as more customer interaction and responsibility. Bob was aware that I was looking for more hours and was supportive of my decision to move on, and he has offered to be a reference for me in the past.

answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: During high school, I provided part-time babysitting and summer childcare for several neighborhood families, typically watching children between ages 2 and 10. Duties included preparing simple meals, organizing outdoor activities, supervising swimming with parental permission, and maintaining a safe environment. I gradually phased out of this work around summer 2013–2014 as I began formal employment at Hawley Market & Deli and Riverbend Family Restaurant, which offered regular hours and payroll employment. I still occasionally help out one of the families if they are in a bind, but it is no longer a formal or consistent job for me.
